title: Topic 193: The Psychology of Gaming Reviews: Why Players Rate Games
content:
# Topic 193: The Psychology of Gaming Reviews: Why Players Rate Games

## ABSTRACT

In an age where a single misplaced pixel can trigger a digital crusade, the act of rating a video game has evolved from a simple consumer metric to a complex psychological phenomenon. This paper bravely ventures into the chaotic wilderness of online gaming reviews, a realm where artistic expression and interactive entertainment are meticulously judged on a scale of one to ten, often with the subtlety of a sledgehammer. We explore the multifaceted motivations that compel players to brandish their keyboards, from the noble pursuit of altruistic guidance to the less-noble, but far more entertaining, desire to watch the world burn. Through a rigorous, albeit frequently hilarious, analysis of player behavior, we dissect the psychological underpinnings of the review process, from the cognitive biases that turn a minor glitch into a federal crime, to the social dynamics that transform a review section into a digital coliseum. This paper will not only illuminate the shadowy corners of the gamer's psyche but also provide a surprisingly insightful commentary on the modern consumer, a creature who, it turns out, is just as likely to be wielding a thesaurus as a controller.

## INTRODUCTION

In the grand, pixelated tapestry of human achievement, few artifacts are as simultaneously revered and reviled as the video game review. It is a battleground of epic proportions, a digital soapbox from which the anonymous masses can dispense their unsolicited wisdom with the fury of a thousand suns. Here, in the hallowed halls of Steam, Metacritic, and their ilk, the fate of multi-million dollar projects is decided not by a council of learned elders, but by a 14-year-old with a Cheeto-dusted keyboard and a bone to pick about the protagonist’s running animation. This paper, dear reader, is your guide to this bewildering world. We shall embark on a perilous journey into the heart of the gamer's mind, a place where logic and reason are often trampled by the stampeding elephants of confirmation bias and the sunk cost fallacy.

Why do they do it? What arcane force compels a seemingly well-adjusted individual to spend three hours crafting a scathing, 2000-word dissertation on the historical inaccuracies of a game about dragons? Is it a selfless act of public service, a noble quest to guide their fellow gamers through the treacherous waters of the digital marketplace? Or is it something more primal, a deep-seated need for validation, a desperate cry into the void of the internet, hoping that someone, somewhere, will acknowledge their exquisitely refined taste in virtual hats? We will explore the full spectrum of motivations, from the altruistic to the absurd, from the player who genuinely wants to help others to the one who is simply seeking to justify the 100 hours they just spent on a game they secretly hate. So, grab your popcorn, silence your cell phones, and prepare to have your faith in humanity both shaken and, just maybe, a little bit restored.

## LITERATURE REVIEW

The academic world, in its infinite wisdom, has not been entirely deaf to the siren song of the video game review. Scholars, armed with their trusty p-values and a concerning amount of caffeine, have ventured into this digital frontier, attempting to map the chaotic landscape of player opinion. Their findings, while often couched in the dry, impenetrable jargon of academia, offer a fascinating glimpse into the gamer's soul.

One of the most hotly debated topics in this burgeoning field is the eternal struggle between the professional critic and the humble user. Kraemer et al. (2024) bravely waded into this fray, discovering that in the video game market, the critic still reigns supreme. Their research suggests that while user reviews are not without their influence, the pronouncements of a seasoned critic, presumably someone who has seen more loading screens than their own family, carry significantly more weight. This is particularly true at the higher end of the rating spectrum, where a glowing review from a trusted source can be the difference between a blockbuster and a bargain bin disaster. The researchers attribute this to the perception that critics, unlike the emotionally volatile masses, employ a standardized evaluation process, a sort of scientific method for determining whether a game is “fun.” The average gamer, it seems, is more likely to be swayed by a well-reasoned argument from a professional than by a thousand screaming voices in the digital wilderness, especially when those voices are all screaming about a slightly-too-shiny sword.

But what of the motivations that drive these digital scribes? Baryak (2022) delved into this very question, emerging from the depths of the internet with a treasure trove of psychological insights. The study identifies a veritable smorgasbord of motivations, ranging from the purely altruistic to the deeply self-serving. At one end of the spectrum, we have the noble “concern for other consumers,” the digital equivalent of a friendly neighbor leaning over the fence to warn you about the dodgy plumbing in the new house down the street. At the other end, we find the more…colorful motivations, such as “exerting power” and “venting negative feelings,” which can be loosely translated as “I paid $60 for this buggy mess, and by God, someone is going to hear about it.” In between, we find a host of other drivers, including the desire for “social benefits” (i.e., making friends in the comments section), the pursuit of “economic rewards” (the holy grail of getting paid to play video games), and the simple, unadulterated joy of “expressing positive emotions,” a rare but beautiful sight in the often-toxic world of online gaming.

## ANALYSIS

Now that we have waded through the hallowed halls of academia, let us don our lab coats and delve into the messy, unpredictable, and frankly, far more interesting world of the gamer's psyche. The act of writing a game review, it turns out, is less a rational evaluation of a product and more a complex psychological ballet, a delicate dance of cognitive biases, social pressures, and deep-seated emotional needs.

At the heart of this phenomenon lies a veritable menagerie of cognitive biases, those delightful little mental shortcuts that help us navigate the world without our brains melting into a puddle of existential dread. **Confirmation bias**, the mother of all biases, is a particularly prominent player in the game review arena. A player who has been eagerly anticipating a game for months, who has devoured every trailer and pre-ordered the collector's edition with the glow-in-the-dark statuette, is not simply playing a game; they are defending a deeply held belief. Every positive aspect of the game is a triumphant validation of their impeccable taste, while every flaw is a minor, easily overlooked wrinkle in an otherwise perfect tapestry. Conversely, a player who goes into a game with a pre-existing negative bias, perhaps due to a controversial marketing campaign or a general distrust of the developer, will actively seek out evidence to support their disdain. A minor bug is not just a bug; it is a damning indictment of the entire industry, a clear sign that the developer is a soulless corporate entity that hates fun and probably kicks puppies in its spare time.

Then there is the **sunk cost fallacy**, that insidious little voice in our heads that whispers, “You’ve already invested 80 hours and your firstborn child into this game, you can’t quit now!” This is particularly prevalent in the world of massively multiplayer online games, where the line between “hobby” and “second job” is often blurred. A player who has spent countless hours grinding for a slightly shinier sword is not just playing a game; they are protecting an investment. A negative review would be an admission that those hours, those precious, irreplaceable hours, were wasted. And so, they write a glowing review, not because the game is necessarily good, but because the alternative is to confront the terrifying existential void that is their life choices.

But it is not all about the individual. The social dynamics of the review section are a fascinating, and often terrifying, spectacle to behold. The **bandwagon effect**, the human tendency to do or believe things because many other people do or believe the same, is in full force here. A game that receives a flood of positive reviews early on is likely to continue receiving positive reviews, as players are swayed by the wisdom of the crowd. Conversely, a game that is subjected to a “review bomb,” a coordinated effort to flood a game with negative reviews, can see its reputation plummet, regardless of its actual quality. This is often driven by a sense of **group identity**, a powerful force that can unite gamers against a common enemy, whether it be a developer who dared to include a female protagonist or a critic who had the audacity to give their favorite game a 7/10.

## REAL-WORLD EXAMPLES

To truly appreciate the beautiful absurdity of the gaming review landscape, one must venture into the wild and observe these phenomena in their natural habitat. The annals of gaming history are littered with cautionary tales and heroic last stands, all played out in the gladiatorial arena of the online review section.

Consider the saga of **No Man's Sky**, a game that promised the universe and initially delivered a slightly-above-average-sized galaxy. The pre-release hype for this game was a force of nature, a tidal wave of anticipation that swept up millions of gamers in its wake. When the game was released and failed to live up to the astronomical expectations, the backlash was swift and brutal. The review sections became a digital wailing wall, a place for shattered dreams and righteous indignation. Yet, in a remarkable turn of events, the developers of No Man's Sky did something that is all too rare in the gaming industry: they listened. They spent years updating and improving the game, adding new features and fulfilling many of their initial promises. And slowly, miraculously, the tide began to turn. The review scores crept up, and the once-scathing reviews were replaced by tales of redemption and second chances. The story of No Man's Sky is a powerful testament to the fact that, while the internet may have the attention span of a goldfish, it also has a surprisingly long memory.

Then there is the curious case of **Cyberpunk 2077**, a game that was so buggy at launch that it was pulled from the PlayStation Store, an event that is roughly equivalent to a restaurant being so bad that it is physically removed from the face of the Earth. The initial reviews were a bloodbath, a symphony of glitches, crashes, and broken promises. Yet, even in the midst of this digital dumpster fire, there were pockets of resistance. Players who were able to look past the technical issues and appreciate the game's ambitious world-building and compelling narrative took to the review sections to defend their beloved, albeit deeply flawed, game. This is a classic example of the **sunk cost fallacy** in action. These players had invested not just their money, but their hopes and dreams into this game, and they were not about to let a few game-breaking bugs stand in the way of their enjoyment.

And who could forget the infamous **“review bombing”** of games like *The Last of Us Part II* and *Captain Marvel*? In these cases, a vocal minority of gamers, often motivated by a cocktail of sexism, homophobia, and a general sense of entitlement, coordinated to flood the games with negative reviews, often before they had even been released. This is a stark reminder of the dark side of group identity, the way in which a shared sense of grievance can be twisted into a weapon of mass digital destruction. It is a sobering thought, but also a strangely comforting one. After all, if a game can survive a review bomb, it can probably survive anything.

## DISCUSSION

So, what can we learn from this chaotic, often contradictory, and always entertaining world of video game reviews? What does it tell us about ourselves, as consumers, as social creatures, and as a species that is willing to spend an entire weekend arguing with a stranger on the internet about the optimal build for a fictional elf?

First and foremost, the psychology of game reviews serves as a powerful reminder that we are not the rational, utility-maximizing creatures that classical economics would have us believe. We are emotional, biased, and deeply social animals, and our purchasing decisions are often driven by a complex interplay of psychological forces that have little to do with the objective quality of the product itself. The same cognitive biases that lead a gamer to write a glowing review for a buggy, unfinished game are at play when we choose a political candidate, a brand of toothpaste, or a life partner. The desire for social validation that fuels the review-bombing of a controversial game is the same desire that drives fashion trends, viral marketing campaigns, and the enduring popularity of reality television.

Furthermore, the world of game reviews offers a fascinating case study in the evolving relationship between consumers and corporations. In an age of unprecedented connectivity, the power dynamic has shifted. Consumers are no longer passive recipients of marketing messages; they are active participants in the creation and destruction of brand reputations. A single, well-written, and brutally honest review can have a greater impact than a multi-million dollar advertising campaign. This has forced companies to become more transparent, more responsive, and, in some cases, more human. The story of *No Man's Sky* is a powerful example of this new reality. A company that was once the poster child for corporate over-promising and under-delivering managed to redeem itself through a combination of humility, hard work, and a genuine willingness to listen to its customers. This is a lesson that companies in all industries would do well to heed.

Finally, the study of game reviews offers a glimmer of hope for the future of online discourse. While the review sections can often be a toxic cesspool of negativity and vitriol, they can also be a place of genuine connection, of shared passion, and of surprisingly insightful commentary. For every troll, there is a thoughtful critic, for every review-bomber, there is a player who is genuinely trying to help others. And in a world that is increasingly divided, that is something worth celebrating.

## CONCLUSION

In conclusion, the act of rating a video game is far more than a simple consumer transaction. It is a window into the human soul, a chaotic and beautiful reflection of our hopes, our fears, our biases, and our deep-seated need to tell the world that, yes, we do have an opinion, and it is a very, very important one. From the altruistic crusader to the vengeful troll, the motivations that drive players to write reviews are as varied and complex as the games themselves. And while it may be tempting to dismiss the world of game reviews as a trivial and often toxic corner of the internet, to do so would be to miss a valuable opportunity to understand ourselves, as consumers, as social creatures, and as a species that is still, after all these years, trying to figure out how to play well with others.
references:
[1] Kraemer, T., Weiger, W. H., & Heidenreich, S. (2024). Do all stars shine the same? Investigating the nonlinear effects of user and critic reviews on video game sales. Journal of Business Research, 188, 115034. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jbusres.2024.115034
[2] Baryak, Ü. K. (2022). What motivates players to write game reviews? [Master's thesis, University of Skövde]. DiVA portal. http://www.diva-portal.org/smash/record.jsf?pid=diva2:1736561
